explanation_md: The variable `m` is initialized with the enumerator `autoMode`. The switch matches the `case autoMode:` arm and returns the string literal `"auto"`. The `<<` operator handles `std::string_view` correctly, so `auto` is printed followed by the newline.

prompt_md: Why is `std::string_view` a better return type than `std::string` for an enum-to-name helper that returns string literals?

options.a: `std::string_view` is the only return type that can hold a string literal; `std::string` cannot.
options.b: Returning a `std::string` allocates and copies on every call, while a `std::string_view` is a non-owning view into a literal that already lives for the lifetime of the program.
options.c: `std::string_view` returns are required by the C++ standard for enum lookups.

explanation_md: String literals like `"red"` have static storage duration — they live for the whole program. A `std::string_view` is a non-owning pointer plus length that views those characters at zero cost. A `std::string` return would copy the characters into a new dynamically allocated buffer on every call, which is pure waste here. Option (a) is wrong (both types can hold the literal), (c) is fabricated, and (d) is also false.

options.a: The function should be declared `inline` to compile.
options.b: The switch is missing `break` after each `return`.
options.c: If `c` ever holds a value outside the named enumerators, the function falls off the end without returning — undefined behavior.
options.d: `std::string_view` cannot be used as a return type.

explanation_md: An unscoped enum variable can hold integer values outside the named enumerators (e.g. via `static_cast`). The switch covers `red`, `green`, and `blue` only; if `c` is `static_cast<Color>(99)`, control reaches the end of the function with no return, which is undefined behavior. Adding `return "unknown";` after the switch fixes it. Option (b) is wrong because `return` already exits the function; the other options are also incorrect.

`petFromName("dog")` matches the second `if` and returns the enumerator `dog`. The variable `p` is initialized to `dog`, whose underlying integer value is 1 (it is the second unscoped enumerator). Printing `p` triggers the implicit enum-to-int conversion, producing `1`.
